- Mansions No One Wants to Buy for Any Price: Kind: captions Language: en - [Narrator] Everyone dreams of owning a mansion, but in reality, it's a dream most of us will never be able to afford, or is it? If you know where to look, you'd be surprised about the incredible mansions you could get on the cheap and even more surprised by why not even millionaires want to buy them. From domineering castles with creepy pasts, to haunting homes suspiciously available for free, let's take a tour of some incredible mansions no one wants to buy for any price. (upbeat ambient music) $0, with a catch. They say that nothing in this world comes free, and when something seems just too good to be true, it usually is. In May, 2019 bargain hunters were astounded when a listing for an impressive seeming four bedroom home on Craigslist went viral for touting a $0 price tag, but there was a catch. The seemingly good-willed seller behind this insane offering was Barb. Kochlin, the owner of the property in Jordan, Minnesota who inherited it from her grandmother, Gail. No one lived in the house, even when it was owned by Gail, and in 2002, the elderly woman made the bizarre decision to relocate the property from the middle of town to the side of the highway. Moving a house is a Herculean task and requires a lot of resources. Relocated properties are usually lifted from under the sills with a jacking system and a network of beams to keep the structure in one piece. Gail's reasons for moving the house were never revealed, but it remained abandoned on the side of the highway for 15 years, until the property fell into Barb's hands in 2017 following Gail's passing. The mini mansions interior is completely empty, except for the remnants of decor from days gone by. Just one glance at those bright orange countertops and cork back splashes enough to tell you that this place needs a significant overhaul when it comes to decoration. Having originally planned to renovate the mansion into a two-story apartment complex, Barb hit a bump in the road when pitching her idea to Jordan City Planning Committee, to turn 508 Broadway into an apartment building, Barb would need to provide ample parking for residence. The problem was that the land the property stood on was zoned for commercial use, meaning that if someone wanted to live in the house in its current location, they'd have to apply to change the land zoning law, which is a long inexpensive process. By April, 2019, Barb had been given an ultimatum by the local government, move the house from the side of the highway, or see it demolished. As a part of her family's history, Barb was determined to save 508 Broadway, but after finding out the costly nature of relocating the property, she decided to list the house for $0 in hope someone else would take care of the moving part. As you can imagine this sort of literal house move doesn't come cheaply and upping and moving homes to new locations can cost as much as $200,000. In addition to the cost of the move, Barb estimated that renovating the home would cost around $150,000, total link to an investment that could cost as much as $350,000. Hardly the $0 of the listing price. That being said, $350,000 is actually around a $100,000 less than the median house price in the area. So it could still be a sound investment for anyone bold enough to undertake the work. While things were looking up for Barb in June, 2019 when she was approached by a property developer, there's been no update on the future of the house since. Thankfully it looks like this highway house is still standing in the very same spot and was still sporting it for sale sign in September, 2021. So the only question is, are you up to the task? Ann Starrett Mansion. They say that home is where the heart is, but sadly, this next mansion has struggled to capture any buyer's heart for many years. Located in the historic town of Port Townsend, Washington, this 1899 beauty was a true labor of love for George Starrett, who built a house for his adored wife Ann. Dubbed the Ann Starrett Mansion, the 5,796 square foot property has 11 bedrooms and nine bathrooms set over two stories. The crowning tribute to Ann is the eight sided dove tower at the mansions entrance that incases and ornate spiral staircase. On the dome ceiling is a solar calendar designed by renowned New York artist, George Chapman, who used Ann Starretts likeness to depict the four seasons and the four virtues, prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance, George and Ann fully enjoyed their beautiful mansion and live there all their married life, passing ownership of the property to their descendants who continued to live there until the 1980s, the Starrett family seat found new caretakers in Bob and Edil Sokol who bought the house in 1986 and turned it into a boutique hotel. After a successful stand as hoteliers, the Sokos put the Starrett Mansion on the market in 2009, but in the 13 years, since then, the mansion has been relisted a surprising seven times to no a veil. While it might seem surprising that a mansion this beautiful would have no interest buyers, some theorized that what lies within the mansion isn't so pretty. Locals believe the Starrett house is haunted by its previous residents, including Ann and George who have allegedly been cited by staff and guests alike. But a loved up ghost couple isn't the only thing new owners could be dealing with, deemed to be the most interactive of the spirits at Starrett House, the ghost of the family nanny is reported to keep a close eye on house guests. The most popular stories are those of critical guests receiving a ghostly smack on the head after complaining about their stay at the house. Currently the 11 bedroom property is listed for a price of $1,500,000, which is a bargain compared with this five bed four bath mansion situated just an eight minute walk away for 1.3 million. Beautiful as it looks, no one's too keen to shack up with Ann Starrett Mansions past or ghostly present, however real those alleged ghosts may or may not be. But considering that 57% of people in the US believe in the existence of ghosts, it's no wonder that haunted houses sit on the market for so long. With that in mind, this seller in Clifton, Tennessee decided to quell superstitions when they listed this property in 2015 with a especially made "Not Haunted" for sale sign. It's unknown why the owners deem these measures necessary to sell this five bedroom lot, but for the low, low price of $99,950, that not haunted edition only serves to make me more suspicious. Nope, no ghost here, totally not haunted. (cartoon swoosh) Foreclosing Fortress. While for most folks, the idea of living in a castle exists only in the realms of imagination, the reality of this next property turned out to be nothing short of a real estate nightmare. Businessman Christopher Mark was the mind behind this one of a kind property based on the fairy castles from his daughter's story books. Complete with a moat and towers raising 126 feet into the sky, this gothic creation took a staggering seven years to build between 2001 and 2008. Dubbed "Chrismark Castle", the property is nestled within 75 acres of sprawling forest and the decor inside transports visitors back in time with its medieval design. There are nine bedrooms and 10 bathrooms all fit for royalty. As well as 12 fireplaces and wooden floors and doors built with over 25 different types of luxurious hardwoods. So what's the catch? You'd think somewhere as magical as this would have plenty of billionaire buyers banging down the door, however, this wonderland has been sitting on the market since 2014, with an asking price of an out of this world, $60 million. Why you might think that the only reason no buyers have chosen to start a chapter at Chrismark Castle is that extortionate price tag. There's a lot more to it than that. Turns out the current owner's private life is far from a fairytale. In 2008, it was reported that Christopher was using the mansion to conceal a suspicious quote, unquote "modeling agency" that reportedly listed women for hire at $125 from the castle. This sketchy venture followed with a messy public divorce from his wife alongside the untimely death of a camel living on the exotic animal refuge on the property, and Christopher also reportedly put his new girlfriend Marina and the child they had together out on the street, refusing to have anything to do with them besides the basic monthly minimum in child support payments. Given that Chris's castle is associated with all of this morally questionable behavior it cast a Gothic darkness over what may have been a charming home, turning into something altogether quite sinister and unnerving. The room and the basement dedicated to pole dancing and other activities involving binding and cages doesn't exactly help the pure and innocent image either. While there may be no go sit Chrismark Castle, it's safe to assume that there are plenty of dark secrets. $1,000 home. Everyone loves a good home makeover, and some say you can't put a price on the result of hard work, but in 2020, this three bed, two bath Victorian era property was listed for just $1,000. The catch is that this authentic property is completely abandoned, and despite its charming exterior, the insides are incomplete disarray. Located in Syracuse, New York, the 2056 square foot house was built in 1890 and features rounded rooms with ornate plastered ceilings that harken back to the 19th century world in which they were constructed. There's a formal dining room and living parlor that could be fit for a very la di da tea party, given a bit of TLC, well, a lot of TLC. Whoever does end up partying with that $1000 will have a lot of crumbling foundations on their hands that will need to undergo a complete reconstruction. That amount of labor won't come cheap. And the property's listing claims that it will require around $200,000 in renovations to restore it to its former glory. Considering the average house in Syracuse is worth around $153,000, this one 1K fixer upper isn't quite the bargain it might seem. While fixer uppers like this are always going to be burying some hidden costs, building a brand doesn't have to cost the earth. Let's be honest, the whole point of buying a mansion is to show off, and the larger you can go, the better. But this next mansion just goes to show that you can have too much of a good thing. This over the top 14 floor residents in Hampshire, England was constructed in the 1880s by wealthy landowner Andrew Turton Peterson. Known as a bit of an eccentric, Andrew developed a keen interest for spiritualism and communicating with people from beyond the grave. One spirit Andrew claimed to have contacted was the renowned 17th century English architect, Sir Christopher Wren, who apparently instructed him to build a large concrete tower, which was pretty odd considering that Wren never used concrete in his designs, but who says people's tastes can't change, right? And so Andrew did as he was told, known as a "folly", large towers like this were pretty popular in the 18th and 19th centuries and were built solely for decoration to be admired by rich homeowners nearby. While it looked pretty, or at least interesting, from the outside, the concrete monolith was home to little more than stairs and pigeons until 1973. When the tower came under new ownership, the grade two listed building was bought by Paul Atkins for just $5,000 who renovated part of the property into a tall skinny mansion for a cool $1 million investment. Paul first turned the tower into a boutique hotel with en suite bedrooms, each taking up an entire floor in the twig like tower before offering the property up for sale more than four decades later. First listed in 2016, the property is conservatively described as a four bed detached house, despite standing at 220 feet and housing 320 steps. If the thought of climbing all those stairs every day wasn't enough to put buyers off, then paying the two and a half million dollar asking price is sure to sour the deal even further. Not only that, but the new owner had best be shock resistant, considering the folly has been struck by lightning (thunder strikes) at least 10 times in its history. With more than five years on the market under its belt, let's hope that this historic structure finds a new owner before mother nature smites it down to rubble. Carleton Island Villa. The thought of owning a mansion on a near private island is a dream harbored by many, but for this next property, things are far from idyllic today. In the late 1800s, businessman William Ozmun Wyckoff was amassing a great fortune from selling the newly invented typewriter. After all that hard work, William needed a break. And in 1894, he said about commissioning a dream vacation home on the picturesque Carlton Island in upstate New York. The property was completed in 1895, and despite being a vacation home, William's 15,000 square foot fortress was completed with a whopping 50 rooms inside. With all that space, as well as an abundance of waterfront views, things were looking bright for the Wyckoff clan's next summer vacation, but things didn't end up so sunny for William as he eerily died in his sleep during his very first night on Carleton Island. The villa was inherited by one of William's sons who quickly sold the property following the tanking of the Wyckoff family fortune during the great depression in the 1920s. The property was sold to energy company, General Electric, who had intended on using the villa to host employee retreats. But sadly, these plans were soon forgotten as World War Two unfurled. Decades flew by and the Carleton Island Villa remained, exposed to the unforgiving elements, as well as numerous lootings over the years. Eventually the Villa came under new ownership by a couple living in a cottage near by the estate who put the mansion on the market in 2012. Despite its colossal size, Wyckoff's mansion was priced at just $495,000, a mere drop in the ocean compared with what you'd think it must be worth. In fact, in the general surrounding area, $480,000 can only buy a quaint four bedroom property. So it might seem odd that no one wants to snap up the 11 bedroom Carlton Villa. However, these days Carlton Island Villa is a ghostly shell of its former self. And some even suspect that the abandoned ruins are haunted by William Wyckoff himself. Regardless of Willie's ghost, the property is regarded as a money pit and is in dangerous disrepair with all 50 rooms crumbling to pieces. The villas desolate location also poses a problem to prospective house flippers as Carlton Island is only accessible by boat. but there's no water or electricity connected to the home, meaning that it would require a lot more than a fresh lick of paint to restore. In fact, the renovations required to get the house in ship shape condition are estimated to cost between 10 and $12 million. The new owners would literally have to start from the ground up as the stone foundations of the property have reached a point of irreversible deterioration and would need to be entirely overhauled. Over 90 years have passed since anyone has lived in the mansion and the Carleton Island Villa vacation home is still waiting for someone to pull it out of the darkness and into the sun once again. Minxiong House. Located in the countryside, just outside of the city of Chiayi, Minxiong Ghost House is one of the most infamously haunted locations in all of Taiwan. Built in 1929, this house was once full of life until at some point in the 1950s, the owners, the Liu family mysteriously upped and left their home behind. The reasons behind the Lou family's swift departure are shrouded in shadows, but locals believe that a haunting is to blame. In Taiwanese culture, upwards of 87% of people reportedly believe in ghosts. And there are many superstitions dictated by a fear of the spirit world. These include beliefs that whistling (whistles softly) can attract evil spirits and that taking the last bus home could result in being abducted by a phantom driver. In the case of the Minxiong house, the leading theory is that the Liu's were haunted by the ghost of a maid they'd mistreated, whose spirit came back to torment them on a nightly basis. The rumors of the hauntings left a home without an owner, and it's been set abandoned ever since. These days, the structure is down to its bare bones and is more of a ruin as opposed to the luxurious house it once was. And it's not just the inside that's transformed, the outside of the red brick structure has been completely overtaken by nature, engulfed by twisting vines and branches. Considering its wrecked appearance, no one in their right mind is looking to part with the $474,901 needed to purchase the ruin, let alone shelling out potentially 100s of 1000s more that would be needed to restore it. It's safe to say this mansions haunted reputation as well as its problematic price tag will likely follow it to the grave. Haunted House on the House! A house with a haunted history is bound to be a tough sell for any realtor. But one agent out there had a nifty trick up their sleeve. In March, 2020 residents of Youngsville, Louisiana got quite the surprise when realtor Sylvia McClain posted this on Facebook, offering an entire house for free. Sylvia disclosed that whoever took it on would be expected to move and restore the 1920 structure, which stood on some land that had been purchased by her neighborhood development firm. The final bill for this house move was set to be in excess of $80,000, but it wasn't the hidden cost that were putting people off, in fact, the comments were flooded with interested buyers, but what Sylvia failed to mention were the reports of an other worldly presence that also allegedly resides in the home. Former resident, Dawn Declout claims that it's occupied by restless spirit often witnessed jangling pots and pans among other creepy unexplained phenomena. While this might not seem like a huge deal to everyone, once interested buyers found out about the supposed haunting, most of them probably withdrew their offers to take the house. Despite the home's ghostly resident, one brave soul did agree to take the mostly free home and relocated the house around 20 miles away. But who knows if the ghost came along for the ride too. If it did it probably won't be long before this moving proper's back on the market again. While nothing in the world of real estate truly comes free, you can get your mitts on plenty of amazing free content here at Be Amazed. Why not put an offer in on those like and subscribe buttons? You can even seal the deal by ringing that bell icon so you're always in the loop when I've got new content on the market. All signs, sealed and delivered, then let's get back to the video. SK Pierce Mansion. When it comes to mansions, they say that bigger is better. And while this next property took 100 men, 18 months to build, it's failure to launch comes down to just one small detail. The elaborate 10 bedroom, 7,000 square foot Gothic mansion was completed in 1875 after being commissioned by businessmen, Sylvester Pierce. Sylvester worked in the furniture business, so it's no surprise that the insides are about as lavish as you can imagine, if not a little eerie, including a steep grand staircase and lavish furnishings. After Sylvester's death in 1888, the mansion was left to his three sons who bickered over the ownership of the mansion for years. Eventually the mansion was turned into a boarding house in an attempt to revitalize the dwindling Pierce family fortune, but this decision saw the mansion play host to some very unsavory activities, illegal gambling, black market trading, frequent visits from women of the night and even some homicides are said to have taken place at the house. By 1965, the Pierce fortune had all but disappeared and ownership of the mansion was transferred outside of the family. Since then, the houses had quite a few residents over the years, some of which have reported some very strange goings on. In 2009, Edwin Gonzales moved into the property with his partner, Lilian Otero, but the pair were driven out of the home by 2011 due to alleged paranormal experiences. While Edwin and Lilian lived there, neighbors reported seeing a little boy in the windows, despite the couple being childless, as well as a dark haired woman who Lilian claims tried to possess her. While all these little anecdotes are creepy to say the least, the couple imparted, their experience via a book deal, which could make the claim seem a lot more or a lot less believable, depending on your perspective. Stories like that of Edwin and Lilian are the reason why locals have dubbed the SK Pierce Mansion "the haunted mansion of the neighborhood". And because of this, not a soul wanted to live there. Guests of the mansion have reported an alarming amount of paranormal activity from chanting voices, apparitions and moving furniture. The property sat on the market for nine years before the seller finally accepted that no one was ever going to buy this ocursed location to live in. Instead they changed tactics and marketed the home as a haunted attraction. In 2015, Edwin and Lilian sold Sylvester's old home to Rob and Allison Conti, the owners of haunted attraction company, Dark Carnival. for a fraction of the mansion's 1.3 million evaluation at just $315,000. Currently guests can only stay in the property after signing a detailed waiver that prevents them from suing Robert over any paranormal activity they may experience. How welcoming. Granot Loma. Homes rented unsellable by their creepiness are one thing, but there are some houses out there with price tags that are just too expensive for buyers to even consider. Dub Granot Loma, This enormous lawn cabin sits on the shores of Lake Superior in Marquette, Michigan. Touting a private marina and 5,000 acres of woodland, the 26,000 square foot cabin was listed for a splintering $40 million in 2015, making it the most expensive house in the state. While you may think that's extortionate, the home was constructed in 1923 by financer, Lewis G Kaufman for 5 million, which is the equivalent of around 82 million today. However, not even billionaires thought it was a steal, and its listing price and the luxurious log cabin was chopped down to $20 million in 2017. Since then, it's still not managed to attract any interest and the price isn't the only thing that has buyers lost in the woods, despite having been designed by a staggering 22 architects, the estate has some pretty dated garish decor and a whole load of insanely creepy taxidermy. Honestly, you'd have to pay me 20 $million to live in this pet cemetery. Although not that I'd say no at that price, I could ship over some of those other haunted homes we've seen with the profit, to form a cozy, creepy commune. Wonderfully nightmarish for the inner goth in all of us. Would you buy any of the mansions we've seen today? Are there any houses no one wants to buy in your neighborhood? building to clean Monday to Friday nights after your nine to five job it takes you an hour to an hour and a half and you take home an extra two thousand dollars a month that is an absolute reality but rates to the top of your Market it which doesn't have to take long you can make six figures a year even working part-time the drawback to this model is that at some point your income will get capped them more accountable to working hard for you especially if you're close by and number three is that you can still make way more money in less time even having just one employee with you here's an   12. @Everybody_Say_Love: Your comment about a 100 year flood zone is a common misbelief. 

A 100-year flood zone is an area with a 1% annual chance of experiencing a flood that equals or exceeds a specific water level (the "base flood"). Contrary to its name, it is a statistical probability rather than a timeline; it does not mean a flood only occurs once every 100 years.

- Seasonal Resident? Why You MUST Think Like a Full-Time Homeowner 🌴 - Coachella Valley Home Watch: Kind: captions Language: en Seasonal living in the Coachella Valley is a dream for many. Beautiful weather, stunning homes, and the freedom to come and go. But while you enjoy the flexibility of part-time residency, your home faces full-time risks. Here's the truth. Your property doesn't know you're a seasonal resident. [Music] The heat, the pests, the storms, and the mechanical systems, they all keep working year round, whether you're there to see it or not. That's why seasonal homeowners need to start thinking like full-time ones. Because a part-time mindset can lead to full-time problems. When you treat your home like a vacation stop, it's easy to postpone small tasks. Maybe the AC filter can wait. Maybe you'll deal with that little leak next time. Maybe the package on the porch will be fine until you come back. But in the Coachella Valley, small things become big problems fast. Rodents move in. Storms damage roofing or landscaping. Irrigation leaks go unnoticed and attract mold or pests. One crack tile today can lead to water damage tomorrow. If you lived there full-time, you'd catch these things early. That's the difference. And that's why making a mental shift. Treating your seasonal home like a primary residence can save you time, money, and stress. Now, you don't have to live in the Coachella Valley year round to take care of your home like you do. That's where a professional home watch service makes all the difference. With regular inspections, trained eyes catch what you can't see from across the country. Appliances are tested, thermostats checked, doors secured, and signs of damage or infestation are documented and dealt with before they escalate. The result, your property stays secure. You stay informed, and when you return, you're not walking into a mess. You're walking into a well-ared for home that's ready for you to relax and enjoy. This is more than just convenience. It's peace of mind. It's protection for your investment. And it's how seasonal residents can take full ownership of their homes even from miles away. So if you're a part-time resident in Palm Desert, Lintita, Indian Wells, or anywhere in the Coachella Valley, it's time to start thinking like a full-time homeowner. Your home deserves that kind of care. And you deserve to enjoy your time here without worry. Contact Coachella Valley Home Watch today and let us help you protect what matters every day of the year.

- Seasonality of housing market in the Northeast region of the USA: Kind: captions Language: en hi there my name is Michael scoutovski as a real estate agent I often get asked about the best time to buy or sell a home well if you're in the Northeast region of the United States it's important to consider seasonality typically the housing market in the Northeast experiences a surge in activity during the summer months this is when families are on vacation kids are out of school and people are generally more relaxed and willing to make a big decision on the other hand the winter months can be a bit slower in terms of housing activity with the Colder Weather and holiday season people tend to put their real estate plans on hold until the spring of course every situation is unique and there are always exceptions to the rule but if you're looking to buy or sell a home in the Northeast it's important to be aware of these seasonal trends now in the beginning of March would be a very good time to start your house search and beat the competition if you have any questions or are looking for guidance on your real estate Journey don't hesitate to reach out to me and you know thank you for watching please like this video and follow me for more real estate tips and news

- 🌴✨New Year Home Reset for Seasonal Homeowners | Coachella Valley Home Watch: Kind: captions Language: en January has a different kind [music] of quiet. The holidays are over, decorations come down, and routines slowly return. For seasonal homeowners, this moment often brings [music] relief. But it's also the most important time to pause and check in on a home that may have been empty, lightly used, or simply overlooked during [music] the busiest weeks of the year. A new year home reset isn't about big renovations or major upgrades. It's about reviewing the small details that quietly [music] keep a home healthy. Because even when a house sits empty, it doesn't stay [music] still. It continues to respond to temperature changes, humidity, and time itself. One of the first things to notice in [music] January is how the home feels inside. After weeks of limited activity, air circulation often [music] drops. Rooms may feel stale, unevenly heated, or slightly off. These subtle shifts are easy to ignore, but they're often the earliest signs that a home needs attention. [music] A space that doesn't feel balanced is usually telling you something. Plumbing systems deserve a fresh look at the start of the year as well. When water isn't used regularly, small [music] leaks can develop quietly. A slow drip under a sink, moisture near a toilet, or a water heater working harder than usual can cause [music] damage long before it's visible. January is when those issues are easiest to catch early. Heating and [music] cooling systems also benefit from a new year check-in. Cooler nights, holiday use, [music] and inconsistent thermostat settings can strain HVAC systems. Uneven temperatures or unfamiliar sounds [music] are often early indicators that something needs adjustment before it turns into a costly repair later in the year. Outside the [music] home, irrigation and landscaping should never be overlooked. Even in winter, timers may have shifted, zones may not be running correctly, and leaks can linger longer in cooler [music] weather. What seems minor in January can quietly escalate by spring if it goes unnoticed. Quiet homes can also attract pests [music] seeking warmth and shelter. Early signs are often subtle. A sound, [music] a smell, a small trace, but catching them now prevents infestations from settling in and spreading [music] over time. Finally, January is a natural moment to review security and access. Doors, [music] windows, and exterior lighting all contribute to a home feeling secure and cared for. Small changes [music] like a light that no longer works or a door that doesn't close the same way matter more than most people realize. A new year home reset isn't about finding problems. It's about [music] confirming stability. It's about starting the year knowing your home is balanced, protected, [music] and ready for the months ahead. Because peace of mind doesn't come from assuming everything is fine. It comes from checking and beginning the year with confidence.

[Music] What is up, guys? I hope you're having a great day today. Welcome back to the Moving to Milwaukee channel. Today, I'm going to continue my expose of other markets throughout the country from the interviews I did in Las Vegas a few months back. Today, I'm talking about seasonal trends in the real estate market. Some markets may be different throughout the year. Some seasons or months may be busier than others. And we're going to dive right into what that is like in some different regions throughout the United States. So to give my market a highlight, I'm out of Wisconsin, so we experience all four seasons up here. Our busy time usually is in the spring and goes through the summer and even into September when the fall hits. Mostly this is the case up here because many people don't want to think about moving in the frigid cold weather. I have lived up here my whole life and I cannot say I blame them for that. When the springtime hits, many people who think of selling are ready to put their home on the market in the most presentable manner. - Home Maintenance Checklist | With Downloadable 1-Pager: Kind: captions Language: en I'm gonna walk you guys through the Everyday Home Repairs maintenance checklist. This is a one pager that's broke up in three different sections. And those sections are according to the frequency for which you're going to be performing these checks or maintenance items. We start off with monthly here. That's going to be your most frequent items. Then we go into the largest column here, which is seasonally or every 3 months. Now, that's going to be spring, summer, fall, winter. and I'm in an area where we have four seasons. So, this kind of favors more of a Midwestern or a four season location. Just understand if you're in the southwest, uh, hot, dry, there's probably going to be a few things that you're going to have to adjust because you could have some different systems within your home. And then finally, the yearly items. Those are things that you're usually checking to make sure you don't get yourself in trouble where you might have a leak in the basement and your sump pump isn't working. and that can lead to large insurance claims or damage that you're going to have to pay out of your pocket. Now, you can look down below this video in the timeline and it's going to be broken up into that monthly. Then we'll go into spring, summer, fall, winter, and finally yearly. So, you can jump to the part you want. Maybe you just want to look at the stuff coming up in the spring. That's fine. You can bookmark this video for future reference. And then at the end, I will show you where you can go and download this one pager so you can hang it on the fridge just as an easy reminder of the things you should be doing around the house. All right, so let's start off with the monthly items. I'm just going to go through give you a little bit more information and context because obviously with just small oneliners, you can't fit that much information into it. So starting off with monthly safety item, checking your smoke and CO detectors. You want to go through and do the test button to make sure they're functioning. Make sure you have batteries in them, even if they're hardwired, that your battery backup is there, and also make sure they're not expired. Remember, they have a shelf life. So, if you have 15year-old smoke detectors, it's time to change them. Test your GFCI outlets. These are going to be in your wet areas of the home. Think bathroom, kitchen, garage. They have the test and reset buttons. So, you can either test them by hitting the test button and it should click and trip and shut off the power to that outlet and then you can press the reset. It should hold a reset and then your power is back on. Alternatively, if you have an outlet tester like this one from Klein Tools, that button in the middle is a GFCI tester. So you can plug this in, trip that. It's going to trip your GFCI if it's working properly. And then you hit that reset and turn the power back on to that receptacle. And then the last monthly item is checking under your kitchen sink or in your bathroom vanities. A lot of people pack a ton of stuff in there. And if you have a slow water leak over time, if you're not actively checking that, you might miss that for months or even longer, it could be deteriorating your cabinet, your subfloor, and causing a lot more damage. So, something to check and maybe all that's need is you just to tighten up the nut on the P trap to avoid a leak. Usually, a very easy fix, but something that if gone unchecked can cause a lot of damage, especially in rental units. That's kind of a classic for rental units. Now, going into seasonal, starting with spring, replace furnace filters. Every 3 months, you should be replacing your furnace filter. Now, this is assuming that you have a forced air furnace or furnace/AC, a central air unit, and that you're using it at a normal frequency. If you barely ever use it, well, your filter is probably not getting exercise too much, and you can probably extend that out. In the spring, I like to wash the outside of my house, especially if you have vinyl siding. In my area, you can get some algae growth and kind of build up on that if gone uncheck. It kind of gets out of control in a hurry. Then repair and replace any damaged screens. This is easier than you expect. Now, in the description below this, I'll link out some of these additional videos I have on my channel that would help you with this, such as I have one on rescreening your window screens. In the spring is when I'm putting down new mulch in the flower beds. Now, if you've been doing bags in the past from Home Depot or Lowe's, just know you might want to call a local landscape center and get a yard or two or three or more dropped off. Often it's cheaper and a lot more convenient to you because it gets delivered right to your home and then you can go right to work. And then the spring is a good time as any to check all the coaul lines in your bathroom, in your kitchen. See if you need to clean those off and reccock just to make sure it's watertight and you don't have mildew or mold buildup. So that's it for spring. Now going on to summer. Again, we're going to replace that furnace filter that will be in every one of these seasonal items. This is a good time to clean and stain your deck. And that's assuming you have a deck with pressuretreated wood. Remove lint from your drier vent. Now, this is where you want to go outside and from the inside making sure the rigid pipe or flexible hose is cleaned out of all lint. This can really build up and that is a fire hazard. That's something you need to be careful on because obviously lint itself is extremely flammable. And then this is a classic for rental units and some homes too that I've toured and that's just tightening up the blades on your ceiling fans. You're probably going to be using those quite a bit in the summer. So just take a Phillips head screwdriver or tighten those up so they aren't drooping from your fan. That happens all the time and you have like this wilting flower fan looking thing. Um, which is not good. Going into fall again, furnace filter. Replace your furnace filter. There's kind of a lot of debate out there. I've done a video lately. I think it's fair to go with a MV rating, an MERV rating of eight. That seems to be the consensus and kind of a happy medium between something that's not going to filter anything out but cause no restriction and something that's going to filter everything out but cause a ton of restriction and possibly damage your furnace. MV 8 is kind of right there in the middle. So, probably the one you want to go with in the fall after the leaves drop. I try to get out there and clean those out before winter. Clean the leaves out of the gutters to make sure the water is going to be able to flow throughout fall, winter, and then going into early spring. I check weather stripping on my door. That's around your door, and then also the bottom seals on my exterior doors, the bottom seals on my garage door. Just anything where it's going to be a cold draft coming up in the winter. And then clean and service your furnace or boiler. Now, I'm all about DIY, obviously, but some of the best money spent could be getting a $65 service charge or a local HVAC company to come out and do a yearly service. They're going to clean the internals up, which can really avoid a failure at an inopportune time. So, when your furnace is working aggressively, when it's 0 degrees Fahrenheit outside, it really sucks if that goes out at that time, right? So the some of that preventative maintenance can save you some pain down the road. All right. And then the final group within seasonal in the winter time. Again, you can guess the first one, right? That's going to be your furnace filter. In the winter time when my trees are dormant, that is when I want to go outside and prune and cut them back depending on what I want to do with the landscaping. It's better to do that when they're dormant opposed to spring or summer when they're growing. Clean your bathroom exhaust fan. Man, bathroom exhaust fans get no love. Those things get really dirty, build up with lint, your fan starts squeaking because it just has all sorts of stuff caked onto it. Again, I have a video on that. Check down in the description and it'll give you more instruction. Tighten up the screws in your door hinges. Now, this can be where you go to tighten it and you notice your screws are stripped and almost all of them are just spinning. Again, look down in the description. You'll see number nine, two and a quarter inch screws is what I like to go with to really give you a lot more meat that you're screwing that into. And that's going to secure your hinges and hopefully ensure that your door stays aligned and your door latch doesn't start to get misaligned. Don't forget your refrigerator also needs some attention. So, we usually push those back into a nook and then they have some condenser coils that can have a lot of dust and buildup. Well, that starts to affect how they actually exchange heat. So, you want to clean those off and make sure they don't have any buildup on the actual coils themselves. And then, believe it or not, in the winter is when you'd want to do a tuneup on your mower. So, you want to avoid the rush in the spring where everybody wants to mow for the first time, but they haven't done anything to the mower forever. So, that's where you're changing out your spark plug, you're changing the oil, you're sharpening your blade, you're cleaning or replacing your air filter, and you're just getting your mower ready for a whole another season of hopefully maintenance-free operation. Water softener, sprinkler systems, pools, hot tubs, septic systems, wells. So if you live out in the country, you have a septic system and well, you have a few more items that you need to be maintaining. So just understand I'm not really including any of those systems. All right, so yearly. First one up is making sure that your main water shut off valve functions in your home. It's not a good situation where you spring a leak and you're scrambling to find the water shut off just to find the water shut off does not work and it does not shut off the water to your home. Now, if you have a ball valve that's just going to be twisting that perpendicular to the actual pipe itself, and sometimes those could get stuck. So, it is good to exercise them once in a while. Turn it off. Go drain down the water at one of your faucets, maybe in the bathroom or the kitchen, basically the faucet on the lowest level in your home, and just make sure the water stops. Now, if you have a globe valve or gate valve where you're doing a multi-turn to turn it off, sometimes those can start to leak over time. It's just good to know that you might need to address that. Maybe you need to get a plumber over or again, you can check one of our many videos which show how to swap that out to a ball valve. Next one is inspecting your crawl space. So, this is only if you have a crawl space. If you're on a concrete slab, does not pertain to you. If you have a basement, I'm assuming you're going down to the basement fairly regular just to make sure there's no standing water, you don't have a moisture issue, you don't have a leak or any other issue popping up. But crawl spaces, many people own homes with a crawl space and they don't go down there for years and years and years and years. And it is common in my area. You go down there and you find quite a few issues. You, the sump pump might be down there but not working. So you have standing water. You might have your duck work actually off and just pumping hot or cold air in your crawl space, wasting a ton of money. So, it's good yearly to take your flashlight down there, crawl around, and just make sure everything is in working order. If you have a fireplace or chimney, this is good time year to get on a yearly cycle of yourself maintaining that. Or again, calling in a professional and just get on their yearly calendar for them to come out, do a service check, which is usually fairly affordable, and make sure you don't have any issues that can cause bigger problems down the road. You want to flush the sediment out of your water heater. So, you'll see a little drain valve at the bottom of your water heater. You'll want to hook up a hose and drain that down. But don't forget, you need to turn off the water coming in. And you need to actually break open the lines so that it doesn't pull a suction. If it pulls a suction, it's not going to really drain anything out. You're not going to get much sediment out. So, again, videos out there. I don't have one on my channel, but there's many out there on how to drain sediment out of your water heater. That type of item is really those type of items that you're trying to extend the serviceable life of the main equipment and some of the main components of your house. This will save you money over time and just save you headaches of of having failures of some of the main systems. You want to test the sump pump in your basement or crawl space. Now, if you're like me, my sump pump runs quite a bit, so I don't need to really test it. That thing's running all the time. But if you're in an area where the water table's maybe a little bit lower, it doesn't run that often until you get a major rain, you can go down with a five gallon bucket and and fill up the pit. So then the float goes up, it trips, the sump pump actually pumps it out. Your gate valve works so water doesn't come back in. You just exercise the system and make sure everything is functioning when you don't need it. Right? It's good to test these things when you don't need it opposed to when you get 3 in of rain over a 24-hour period. home perimeter walk. So, going outside your home, looking at your foundation base, looking for any cracks, looking for any issues, maybe your downspouts are off. So, if water being dumped at the base of your foundation, which is not ideal, making sure the gas line coming into your home, the cable, the coax cable coming into your home, making sure those are all sealed. I usually seal large holes with spray foam and let it expand out. And then smaller holes. I'm just using like a clear silicone to seal that off. And then lastly, fire extinguisher. Make sure you have one. Make sure you know where it's at and it's in a good location. And make sure that that pressure gauge is in the operable range. Make sure it's it's pressurized and it's not dropped all the way to zero. - Inside The World’s Most Secret House Built Into a Mountain (House Tour): Kind: captions Language: en Of all the places on this piece of coast this cove is the prettiest little secret spot. And the whole house looks down here at&nbsp; this tiny crescent of beach with the surf rolling. It's extremely private, nobody knows where&nbsp;you are. Nobody knows&nbsp; where they'll find you. My name is Andrew Patterson from Patterson&nbsp;Associates, this is a honeymoon cottage called&nbsp;Seascape. It sits really discreetly in&nbsp;this tiny cove facing into the Southern Ocean and it's partially buried underground. It's just two&nbsp; rooms, bedroom, living room and a bathroom. The bedroom is angled to look&nbsp;down onto the beach while the&nbsp;living area looks directly at the comb. The bathroom area looks directly at&nbsp; the comb but the outdoor fireplace looks directly at the beach and&nbsp; then there's this undulating glass wall and it's a 180° view of powerful waves coming in just meters away from you. The Cove is very remote&nbsp;and most people access it by helicopter. But you can also access it on a long&nbsp; winding farm track and the building doesn't try and command the cove it just sits discreetly&nbsp; off to one side and the cove's surrounded by these rock faces and the intention is&nbsp; that the building just formed another rock face. So say you're out in a boat which&nbsp; is really the only way to see this place the building kind of blends in to&nbsp; the rock and scapa surrounding the cove and it was really the love&nbsp; of the spot that made the building happen. So the building's&nbsp; designed for just two people and it's just a place for&nbsp; them to be safe and warm. It's about the simple rituals of life&nbsp;of a couple, bathing, sleeping, eating and connecting. It's a really romantic place because&nbsp; the dolphins visit the bay nearly every day and the breakers roll in, the storms come and&nbsp; go and sometimes the air turns like crystal in a beautiful blue day and the sun beats down. You&nbsp; can imagine a couple shipwrecked and swimming to shore and coming here and discovering this cave&nbsp; and lighting a fire on the floor and making a nice warm bed and exploring the next day and this&nbsp; building tries to hark back to that very simple experience. But I think the interesting&nbsp; thing for me is the design is actually the plan of two smaller buildings combined. One plan has the glazing facing the comb and one plan focuses on the beach so what&nbsp; happens these two combine and it creates a really rich experience for people and the&nbsp; wonderful thing about having a southerly view is that it's illuminated in front of&nbsp; you so you're the audience looking at the view when you're looking directly South&nbsp; and this view is particularly dramatic because we are out into the seascape. So the building is really a vehicle to allow people to actually connect with nature, with&nbsp; the natural environment with Mother Earth. So in this one we tried not to be&nbsp; influenced by things that have gone before. We really tried to grow the design directly out&nbsp; of the site because if a building looks like it belongs in its location people can relax and&nbsp; commune with themselves and nature and believe me it was a lot of love and connection to get&nbsp;a building on this spot. Everything for&nbsp;the building, all the materials, the rock the&nbsp;timber, the flooring was all taken from the site itself. Some of the only things that were&nbsp; imported were metal like stainless steel but even the earthquake-proof and storm-proof steel&nbsp; joinery that you can see was made locally in the area. The building itself is tiny and it's&nbsp;in a high earthquake zone. New Zealand being the land of earthquakes and there&nbsp; was an earthquake during construction. So the building's got an almost metre thick, a poured in-situ concrete roof like a shield which protects the building in case&nbsp; of earthquake from falling land slips and screeds. There was no way of getting a&nbsp; concrete truck here in this remote spot. So all of the concrete had to be mixed&nbsp; by hand and poured and shuttered by hand. And then the cladding on that concrete is the&nbsp; local rock from the farm and it's the same rock as you can see that the actual landscape&nbsp; and cove is made out of and I like the way it helps the building be part of this cove and grow&nbsp; out of this cove and sort of settle into this cove. So the wood is a reference to the&nbsp;ecology of the peninsula and what's been lost. And here we've just used it sparingly as a&nbsp; liner and also as a reference to replanting and reforestation program that you can see in its&nbsp;infancy at the moment. The philosophy&nbsp;of our practice in working on sites like this&nbsp;is kind of a deep New Zealand philosophy that&nbsp;&nbsp; we are the children of the sky father and&nbsp; an earth mother and we're the product of this conjugal embrace of sky and earth and&nbsp; that makes us a natural part of the planet. And that comes with it a responsibility to look&nbsp; after Mother Nature and look after Mother Earth but it also gives you confidence that we're&nbsp; allowed to be here, we're a natural part of the planet and our buildings are an expression&nbsp; of that. So I think the biggest joy for me about the building is actually staying&nbsp; here you know I love to stay here and it's&nbsp;&nbsp; like a cook saying you know they love to eat&nbsp; their own food I know and normally like a&nbsp;&nbsp; cook sometimes an architect doesn't but I&nbsp; get a lot of joy when I've stayed here. I'm just so pleased that so many&nbsp; couples have visited this place as a way of celebrating and connecting with&nbsp; each other and connecting with nature.
